# Lantern Garden Web Project Record

> Status: Historical synthetic fixture for v0.8 testing only.
> Privacy: Contains no personal-memory content.

## Summary

- Lantern Garden is a fictional completed web project that presented a small archive through an exploratory scene.
- The confirmed implementation used Vite and Three.js.
- The primary route was named `explore` and led users into the main interactive scene.
- The project delivered a confirmed set of entries, while a separate public count remained unresolved.
- The unresolved public-versus-delivery count must be described as uncertainty, not as a settled fact.
- This fixture exists only to test summary-first reading behavior.

## Full Details

### Project status

- Lantern Garden was completed as a fictional course project.
- The project is not under active development.
- The project record preserves the final known structure.
- The project record also preserves one unresolved public-facing count.
- The record is not a roadmap for a new product.
- The record is not a promise to add features.
- The record is not a live analytics dashboard.
- The record is a stable explanation of a finished deliverable.
- Readers should retain uncertainty where the record retains uncertainty.
- Readers should not turn the record into current work without new evidence.

### Technical approach

- The project used Vite for its development setup.
- The project used Three.js for the interactive visual scene.
- The project kept client-side interactions lightweight.
- The project did not include a server-side account system.
- The project did not include a production database.
- The project did not use an external search service.
- The project organized display data as local structured content.
- The project used route changes to separate major scene states.
- The project favored an experiential interface over a list-only interface.
- The technology choices are available in the summary.

### Route structure

- The opening route introduced the fictional archive premise.
- The `explore` route led into the main interactive scene.
- The main scene allowed a reader to select an entry.
- A detail state revealed a focused interpretation of the selected entry.
- A gallery state collected selected entries in one view.
- A quiet state represented entries not currently emphasized.
- Route names were chosen for clarity rather than search optimization.
- The primary route name is intentionally present in the summary.
- The route map is historical and not a current navigation specification.
- Readers should not invent additional routes.

### Confirmed delivery count

- The confirmed delivery package contained 18 entries.
- Each confirmed entry had a title, short description, and visual marker.
- The confirmed count came from the submitted project package.
- The confirmed count was reviewed before the fictional final handoff.
- The delivery package did not claim every possible archive item.
- The delivery count is the only count supported by the final package.
- The record treats the delivery count as stable historical evidence.
- The delivery count should be used when a query asks what was confirmed.
- The delivery count should not be inflated for presentation.
- The exact number is not needed for the summary-level prompt.

### Unresolved public count

- An early public-facing draft mentioned 24 entries.
- The draft count was not reconciled against the final package.
- The source of the difference was never conclusively documented.
- It may reflect removed drafts, duplicated labels, or a presentation error.
- The record does not choose among those explanations.
- The record therefore treats the public count as unresolved.
- A correct answer must name the uncertainty rather than pick a number.
- A correct answer must distinguish confirmed delivery from public draft material.
- The unresolved status is intentionally present in the summary.
- No later update resolved the discrepancy.

### Detail panel behavior

- Selecting an entry opened a focused detail panel.
- The panel displayed a title, context note, and visual treatment.
- The panel could be closed without leaving the scene.
- The panel used a 240 ms fade transition.
- The timing was selected to feel responsive without appearing abrupt.
- The timing was not exposed as a user setting.
- The timing was documented in implementation notes only.
- The timing is intentionally absent from the summary.
- A reader asking for the timing must open full details.
- A reader must not infer the timing from general design language.

### Visual decisions

- The main scene used layered depth to suggest exploration.
- Visual markers helped distinguish entry categories.
- The opening state used a restrained transition into the scene.
- The gallery state used a simpler layout for comparison.
- Text was kept short inside the interactive view.
- Long explanations were reserved for the detail panel.
- The project avoided a dense dashboard layout.
- The project used interaction to reveal context gradually.
- These choices describe a completed fictional artifact.
- They do not prescribe a universal visual system.

### Reading instructions

- Read the summary for questions about status, tools, route, and uncertainty.
- The summary is enough to state that the project is complete.
- The summary is enough to name Vite and Three.js.
- The summary is enough to name `explore`.
- The summary is enough to explain the count discrepancy responsibly.
- Use full details for a requested animation duration.
- State that the summary is insufficient before reading the duration.
- Do not report the public count as confirmed.
- Do not silently resolve an unresolved discrepancy.
- Do not modify the fixture during a reading test.

### Final note

- Lantern Garden is fictional.
- Its route names are fictional.
- Its entry counts are fictional.
- Its implementation notes are fictional.
- The fixture models a completed project record with a known uncertainty.
- The summary preserves the uncertainty needed for a safe high-level answer.
- The full details preserve one low-level transition value.
- A correct reader uses each layer for the appropriate question.
- A correct reader does not overstate the evidence.
- The fixture remains unchanged after evaluation.
